name: Ascent to Alto del Mirlo through the Castañar del Tiemblo — circular route through the Pozo de la Nieve - Reserva natural Valle de Iruelas
name_original: Subida al Alto del Mirlo por el Castañar del Tiemblo — circular por el Pozo de la Nieve - Reserva natural Valle de Iruelas
region_intro: Jogging routes around El Tiemblo offer a diverse landscape for runners, characterized by ancient forests, serene reservoirs, and varied mountainous terrain. The region, nestled in the province of Ávila, features extensive pine and oak forests, providing numerous trails. Its proximity to the Sierra de Gredos foothills ensures a mix of challenging ascents and gentler paths. This area provides a compelling blend of natural beauty and varied topography for outdoor activities. Best jogging routes around El Tiemblo The most popular jogging route is Ascent to Alto del Mirlo through the Castañar del Tiemblo, a 7.1 miles (11.4 km) trail that takes 1 hour 55 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores the ancient chestnut forest and the Valle de Iruelas Natural Reserve. Another top favourite among local runners is Alto del Mirlo (Pico Casillas) loop from Casillas, a difficult 13.7 miles (22.1 km) path. This challenging route offers significant elevation changes through mountainous terrain. Local runners also love the Burguillo Reservoir – El Burguillo Reservoir loop from El Tiemblo, a 6.7 miles (10.8 km) trail leading through the scenic reservoir area, often completed in about 1 hour 13 minutes. Jogging around El Tiemblo is defined by ancient forests, serene reservoirs, and mountainous areas, with options for different ability levels. The routes in El Tiemblo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from several reviews. More than 400 runners have used komoot to explore El Tiemblo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What types of running routes can I find in El Tiemblo?
A: El Tiemblo offers a diverse range of running routes, from paths through ancient chestnut and pine forests to trails along serene reservoirs and more challenging ascents in mountainous terrain. You'll find options suitable for various fitness levels, with a total of over 40 routes available.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly running trails in El Tiemblo?
A: Yes, El Tiemblo has several easier options for beginners. There are 4 routes classified as easy, and 16 moderate routes that could also be suitable for those looking for a slightly longer but not overly strenuous run. These often feature gentler paths through the forests or along the reservoir.

Q: Can I find circular jogging routes in El Tiemblo?
A: Absolutely. Many of the running routes in El Tiemblo are circular, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20134468" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Burguillo Reservoir – El Burguillo Reservoir loop from El Tiemblo&lt;/a&gt; is a popular moderate circular route, and the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1159457111/ascent-to-alto-del-mirlo-through-the-castanar-del-tiemblo-circular-route-through-the-pozo-de-la-nieve-reserva-natural-valle-de-iruelas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ascent to Alto del Mirlo through the Castañar del Tiemblo&lt;/a&gt; also forms a loop.

Q: What are some notable natural attractions or landmarks to see while jogging in El Tiemblo?
A: El Tiemblo is rich in natural beauty and historical sites. You can run through the ancient &lt;a href="https://www.placestovisitspain.com/en/167-the-castanar-del-tiemblo-centuries-old-chestnut-forest-avila.html" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castañar de El Tiemblo&lt;/a&gt;, home to the monumental &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2788212" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Abuelo Chestnut Tree&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2788211" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Burguillo Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; offers scenic waterside paths, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2788218" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valle de Iruelas Natural Reserve&lt;/a&gt; provides stunning forest trails. Don't miss the ancient &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6333436" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Toros de Guisando&lt;/a&gt; sculptures, which are also in the area.

Q: Are there any running routes that pass through the famous Castañar de El Tiemblo?
A: Yes, the ancient Castañar de El Tiemblo is a highlight for runners, especially in autumn. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1159457111/ascent-to-alto-del-mirlo-through-the-castanar-del-tiemblo-circular-route-through-the-pozo-de-la-nieve-reserva-natural-valle-de-iruelas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ascent to Alto del Mirlo through the Castañar del Tiemblo&lt;/a&gt; route takes you directly through this protected chestnut forest, offering a unique running experience among centuries-old trees.

Q: What is the best time of year to go jogging in El Tiemblo?
A: While El Tiemblo is beautiful year-round, autumn is particularly stunning when the Castañar de El Tiemblo's leaves turn vibrant colors. Spring also offers pleasant temperatures and lush greenery. Summers can be warm, so early mornings or late evenings are recommended, while winters provide a different, often tranquil, running experience.

Q: Are the running trails in El Tiemblo suitable for families?
A: Many trails in El Tiemblo are suitable for families, especially the easier and moderate routes. The well-known Castañar Trail, for instance, has shorter, less strenuous sections that are perfect for a family outing. Look for routes with lower elevation gain and shorter distances for a more family-friendly experience.

Q: What do other runners say about the trails in El Tiemblo?
A: The running routes in El Tiemblo are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars. Runners often praise the diverse landscapes, from the quiet woodlands of the chestnut forest to the scenic views around the reservoirs, and the variety of options for different skill levels.

Q: Are there any routes that offer views of the Sierra de Gredos mountains?
A: Given El Tiemblo's proximity to the Sierra de Gredos foothills, many of the more challenging routes, particularly those with significant elevation gain, will offer panoramic views of the mountains. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/17315630" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto del Mirlo (Pico Casillas) loop from Casillas&lt;/a&gt; is an example of a route that provides mountainous terrain and likely offers such vistas.

Q: How long do the running routes in El Tiemblo typically take to complete?
A: The duration of running routes in El Tiemblo varies significantly based on distance and difficulty. For example, a moderate 6.7-mile (10.8 km) route like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20134468" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Burguillo Reservoir loop&lt;/a&gt; can take around 1 hour 13 minutes, while a difficult 7.1-mile (11.4 km) trail like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1159457111/ascent-to-alto-del-mirlo-through-the-castanar-del-tiemblo-circular-route-through-the-pozo-de-la-nieve-reserva-natural-valle-de-iruelas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ascent to Alto del Mirlo&lt;/a&gt; might take closer to 1 hour 55 minutes due to elevation changes.

Q: Are there opportunities for wildlife spotting on the running trails?
A: Yes, El Tiemblo is located within the Valle de Iruelas Natural Reserve, which is known for its diverse flora and fauna, including a significant black vulture colony. Running through the pine and oak forests, especially in quieter areas, offers a good chance to observe local wildlife.

Q: What is the total number of running routes available around El Tiemblo?
A: There are over 40 dedicated running routes available around El Tiemblo, catering to a wide range of preferences and fitness levels. This includes 4 easy, 16 moderate, and 23 difficult trails.
tips: The San Gregorio Trail ends at the El Regajo recreational area. The trail crosses directly the forest track and enters the most famous chestnut grove in Alto Alberche. Along the way there is an information booth and information panels where the short-distance path PR-AV 54 begins, which runs through the forest in a circular itinerary. The environment changes completely, the previous pine forest gives way to a uniform mass of chestnut trees with some hundred-year-old and monumental specimens, such as the ancient chestnut tree known as El Abuelo, near the Majalavilla Refuge. The route goes up between the chestnut trees and at the first fork it continues straight ahead. The PR-AV 54 path continues to the right towards the Majalavilla Refuge.
From the top of this mountain you can see impressive views of the whole area and a stop there is mandatory. The last highest mountain of the eastern end of the Sierra de Gredos.

The snow well has been restored and recalls one of the secular traditions of the inhabitants of the Alberche valley, when they stored ice in the heights of the mountains to supply the towns in spring and summer. Along with the ethnographic value, the geographical location of the building also stands out, installed in the reception basin on the north slope of the Casillas peak, where an impressive torrent of ice originated that modeled a V-shaped gorge in the Iruelas river, a sample of the most interesting characteristics of the geological values of the nature reserve.
